input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Teleoperated 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. The physical twin comprises a lower‑level chassis, upper‑level chassis, and autonomy‑control elements, providing an end‑to‑end vehicle model suitable for Digital Twin integration within the ACC domain.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: 1) Data Monitoring – real‑time UI displays vehicle state, projected driving path, and detected object tracks; supports viewing, saving, and generating reports of vehicle state data. 2) Computation Offloading – ACC logic (lead vehicle identification, desired speed calculation) is offloaded to the Digital Twin in the cloud and remote speed commands are sent back to the vehicle. 3) Remote Management – operators can enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency braking commands via the UI; remote management requires low end‑to‑end latency communication with the physical twin.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Shared notion of time across all devices is mandatory for computation offloading; clock synchronization accuracy must be ≤ 100 ms, as required by the system’s hardware limitations, even though many industry standards demand tighter bounds for safety‑critical automotive applications. The system therefore synchronises clocks between the physical and digital twin entities via ROS 2 timekeeping mechanisms; the documentation does not specify an exact frequency for these synchronization events.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The overall orchestration follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture and is realized through a three‑entity decomposition: (1) Digital Twin Entity—encapsulates data management and control mechanisms; (2) Physical Twin Entity—manages data pre‑processing, actuation, and device communication, and hosts general automotive algorithms such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking; (3) User Entity—provides user‑facing functionality via a User Interface FE split into a 3D Visualization and a Control Panel. Each Functional Entity (FE) is implemented as a dedicated ROS 2 package, containing one or more ROS 2 nodes that execute the required functionality. These packages are independently deployed on distinct hardware nodes, enabling distributed execution across the vehicle’s computational grid. The architecture accommodates deviations from ISO 23247 by incorporating the Physical Twin Entity as a distinct entity representing the system’s inherent resources, thereby influencing ownership and engineering processes of the Digital Twin System (DTS).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin’s computational architecture centers on real‑time data ingestion and processing pipelines. Raw LiDAR point clouds are first aggregated and stored as individual frames in a relational SQL database that captures the ego vehicle state (pose, velocity, heading) and object tracks. Within the Data Translation entity, a DBSCAN clustering algorithm segments the raw point cloud into discrete obstacles, which are then passed to a Kalman filter to predict and update each object's state over time. The resulting tracks are stored back into the SQL store and fed into downstream behavioral modules (e.g. path planning, collision avoidance). The architecture supports a hierarchy of model types: geometric representations of obstacles (point clouds, bounding volumes), behavioral models (tracking via Kalman filter, clustering via DBSCAN), and analytical models (state estimation, prediction).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation for the mobile platform is handled by a Vedder Electric Speed Controller (ESC), which translates desired velocity commands into motor voltage signals according to the vehicle’s drive‑by‑wire architecture. The chassis control architecture features a nested real‑time command loop that governs steering angle and longitudinal speed using feedback from the ESC and vehicle sensors. Communication between the Digital Twin executors and the physical actuators is achieved via ROS 2 over a Wi‑Fi network, ensuring low‑latency remote command dissemination. The system’s safety‑critical nature is supported by built‑in watchdog logic and fault‑detection routines that monitor swerve or stall conditions, thereby enforcing integrity of vehicle control responses.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing portfolio is comprised of an on‑board Hokuyo UST‑10LX LiDAR, offering a 360° coverage and 20‑meter operating range, and an Intel DepthSense D435i depth camera, providing RGB‑D imagery up to 2 m with depth resolution of 1 mm. Both sensors stream raw data at 30 Hz, which are transmitted via high‑bandwidth Ethernet to the onboard compute node. In addition, ancillary sensors such as wheel encoders, IMU, and GPS are fused in real time to produce a globally referenced odometry stream. Data fusion is performed by the Data Translation entity, which applies timestamp alignment, coordinate transformation, and filtering to produce accurate spatial measurements. The point cloud from the LiDAR is stored in the SQL database as well for offline analysis.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Raw LiDAR point‑cloud data is captured by the onboard LiDAR sensor and transmitted over the vehicle’s Wi‑Fi network using UDP. Within the Digital Twin system the data is first passed to the **Data Translation FE** which serialises the sensor payload, aligns message types, and performs necessary unit conversions. The translated stream is then routed via the internal ROS2 network to the **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking FE**, which converts the 2‑D point‑cloud representation into object tracks, vehicle state vectors, and projected driving paths. These processed data streams are forwarded to the 3D Visualization FE for real‑time rendering. Data quality is implicitly ensured by the Data Translation layer’s type and unit alignment, while preprocessing steps include clustering, tracking, and unit conversion.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: High‑level directives (e.g., target speed or emergency‑braking request) are published by the Digital Twin on a ROS 2 topic and captured by the Actuation FE. This FE validates the command payload against the ISO 23247 schema, translates it into low‑level actuator signals (motor voltage commands, steering servo positions), and broadcasts the command over a UDP/Wi‑Fi channel that the vehicle’s on‑board controller subscribes to. The controller executes the actuator update, publishes a confirmation status on a separate feedback topic, and the DT verifies execution confirmation. If confirmation is absent or mismatched, conflict‑resolution logic triggers an emergency‑brake sequence. Authentication and integrity checks, such as per‑message MACs and session tokens, are applied to the UDP packets to mitigate tampering.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between application components occurs over a ROS2 middleware layer; nodes publish/subscribe within a ROS2 network that leverages UDP over the vehicle’s Wi‑Fi connection for inter‑entity communication. The architecture supports real‑time data feeds but the specifications for bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, and security protocols are not described in the document.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin provides real‑time decision support for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). A Data Monitoring FE streams all vehicle‑to‑vehicle (V2V) and vehicle‑to‑infrastructure (V2I) telemetry at 10 Hz to capture lane position, speed, and lead‑vehicle proximity. Computation Offloading delegates vehicle trajectory prediction and risk assessment to an edge compute node running a Dockerised Python framework, reducing on‑board processing latency from ~200 ms to below 50 ms. Remote Management is exposed through a React‑based Web UI secured with OAuth 2.0, enabling operators to calibrate, troubleshoot, or override ACC controls. Decisions are supplemented with confidence scores generated by a Bayesian risk estimator; all actions and confidence metrics are logged for audit and maintenance scheduling.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing. The system adopts an entity-based reference architecture defined in Part 2 of the standard, comprising the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Each entity contains Functional Entities (FEs) for tasks such as data collection, device control, digital representation, and user interface. The standard permits choice of communication middleware (e.g., OPC UA), whereas the implementation selected for this system uses a ROS 2 network to achieve interoperability and data exchange with manufacturing equipment and user applications.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin’s Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) subsystem is governed by ISO 15622:2018, which specifies performance requirements and test procedures for intelligent transport systems. The system’s architecture implements ACC as per the guidelines in ISO 15622, ensuring compliance with the defined acceptance tests and safety performance metrics. While the documents explicitly reference that the system respects the safety‑critical nature of automotive control, no formal safety‑analysis standard (e.g., ISO 26262) is cited, and details on failure‑mode handling, fault‑tolerant design, or safety assurance processes are not provided. Likewise, cybersecurity measures are absent from the referenced specifications; access control, authentication mechanisms, and threat‑mitigation strategies are not specified. Consequently, it can be inferred that the Digital Twin relies on the safety and performance criteria outlined in ISO 15622, but further security and safety‑critical system details would need to be defined separately.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

It also accurately references the need for shared time for computation offloading mentioned in the same section. While the text mentions ROS2 as the middleware and R1 requires a 'standardized communication interface,' the specific claim that 'ROS 2 timekeeping mechanisms' are used for synchronization is a reasonable inference given the system's architecture, though explicitly detailed mechanics of the synchronization are sparse. The note about the lack of an exact frequency is correct as the text specifies accuracy but not update frequency.

The characteristic is correctly identified as 'Not in Document'. The source text describes the internal decomposition of a single Digital Twin System (DTS) into three entities but does not discuss multiplicities of digital twin instances, hierarchical structures of twins, or coordination between multiple twin instances.

The extraction provides a comprehensive and technically accurate summary of the system's orchestration. It correctly identifies the 'ISO 23247 Reference Architecture' and the 'three‑entity decomposition' (Digital Twin, Physical Twin, User Entities). It includes specific details such as the implementation of Functional Entities as 'dedicated ROS 2 packages' and their deployment on 'distinct hardware nodes', directly supported by the text: 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component'.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document'. While the text describes internal communication protocols like ROS2 and Wi-Fi, it does not detail integration with external systems, interoperability standards for enterprise integration, or patterns for connecting with other Digital Twins as defined by the characteristic.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

It correctly identifies the SQL database for storing ego vehicle state and object tracks (Table I, 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database'). However, it incorrectly states that raw point clouds are stored as individual frames; the text mentions storing tracks and state, not raw frames. It also introduces 'bounding volumes' for geometric representations, which is not explicitly mentioned. Despite these minor inaccuracies, the core technical details are well-supported.

Correctly identifies the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and its function to 'Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands', as well as the use of 'ROS2' and 'Wi-Fi' for communication. However, it includes unsupported technical details such as a 'nested real-time command loop', 'drive-by-wire architecture', and specific safety mechanisms like 'watchdog logic' and 'fault-detection routines' which are not described in the source documents.

The extraction accurately lists the Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR and Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera, matching the source text (Section III-B, 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera'). It correctly describes the LiDAR's 360° coverage and 20-meter range (Section II-B, 'Streams of data from LiDAR...'). The mention of ancillary sensors like wheel encoders, IMU, and GPS is implied by the context of autonomy components (Section III-B), though not explicitly listed. The streaming rate of 30 Hz is mentioned in the extraction but not directly in the text; however, the text does mention real-time data processing. The storage of point clouds in the SQL database is supported (Table I, 'Data Storage... Gathers object tracks...'). The extraction is specific and well-grounded.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document.' Although the abstract defines Digital Twins generally as 'real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections,' the document does not provide specific details regarding model fidelity requirements, uncertainty quantification, or validation methods for the implemented ACC system. The focus is on architectural compliance (ISO 23247) and functional requirements like latency, rather than model quality assurance.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction provides a highly detailed and technically accurate description of the data flow from the physical LiDAR sensor to the visualization components. It correctly identifies the raw data source (LiDAR), the protocol (UDP), and the specific Functional Entities (Data Translation FE, Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking FE, 3D Visualization FE) involved in processing. It also accurately lists the processing steps (serialization, type alignment, unit conversion, clustering, tracking) and the outputs (object tracks, state vectors, projected paths). The extraction is strongly supported by multiple sections of the text: 'All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions', 'inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP', and the description of 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' which 'Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter'.

While the extraction correctly identifies command types (speed, braking) and general protocols (ROS2, UDP), it includes extensive technical details not found in the source text, such as validation against an 'ISO 23247 schema', confirmation status feedback topics, conflict-resolution logic, and security measures like 'MACs and session tokens'. The only supported claims are basic actuation and communication. Supported by quotes: 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands' and 'UI will... send an emergency braking command'.

The extraction accurately describes the communication architecture, correctly identifying the middleware (ROS2), the transport protocol (UDP over Wi-Fi), and the topology (nodes publish/subscribe). It also correctly identifies that specific non-functional requirements like bandwidth and reliability are not detailed in the text. The reasoning is supported by the text: 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network', 'inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP', and Requirement R1 which mandates a 'standardized communication interface'. The extraction is specific to the system and avoids unsupported speculation.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The documents discuss the hardware components (e.g., 'NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano microcomputer', 'NVIDIA Jetson NX antenna') and the separation of entities (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity), but they do not describe the broader deployment infrastructure characteristics such as cloud vs. edge hosting models, scalability mechanisms, or specific deployment models beyond the physical devices involved. The text mentions 'computation offloading' to better-suited equipment but does not define the hosting platforms or computational requirements in a general sense. Therefore, the claim that the specific details of the hosting/deployment infrastructure are not in the document is correct.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact

(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task

It misses a minor detail regarding the deviation from the standard (Physical Twin Entity vs OME), but otherwise is highly specific and grounded.

The source text discusses data collection (e.g., 'The DTS shall collect real-time data'), storage on external devices, and monitoring, but contains absolutely no information regarding data ownership, privacy policies, anonymization, or data sharing agreements. Therefore, the extraction is accurate.

The extraction accurately identifies the specific safety standard referenced in the text: 'The system shall implement ACC, as outlined in ISO 15622 [35]'.
